dependencies: Certain key materials and proprietary equipment used in manufacturing are sole-sourced or available only from a limited number of suppliers
Significant geographical concentration of manufacturing plants in Asia Pacific region
Reliance on global supply chain for key components and capabilities
Dependence on uninterrupted power sources, industrial water, precious metals and various batch materials
risks: Supply chain disruptions due to transportation interruptions, labor strife or capacity constraints
Shortage of semiconductor components or other critical parts
Volatility in availability and cost of materials, equipment and services
Difficulties in obtaining required import or export approvals
Natural disasters, climate change impacts, or geopolitical events affecting operations
Single-site manufacturing locations for specialized products with no replacement capacity available
Concentration risk in Asia Pacific manufacturing facilities

regulatory_risks: Compliance with multiple legal and regulatory requirements across jurisdictions increases costs
Data privacy requirements and employment and labor laws in multiple countries
Anti-competition regulations and prohibitions on payments to governmental officials
Import and trade restrictions and export requirements
U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act and similar foreign anti-corruption laws
Environmental regulations including pollution-control, greenhouse gas emissions, and climate change regulations
Changes in tax laws, regulations and international tax standards including OECD guidelines
Pillar Two Framework requiring 15% global minimum effective tax rate
State, federal and foreign pollution-control regulations including Superfund designations
FDA and other regulatory agency requirements for life sciences products
Intellectual property laws and patent protection enforcement challenges
Growing anti-competition legislation and enforcement activity
Trade policies including tariffs, import quotas, and capital controls

sanction_risks: Increased trade restrictions between the U.S. and China and other countries
Tariffs and trade barriers affecting international operations
Government protectionist policies and sovereign risks
Potential for trade tensions and retaliation between governments
Export controls and sanctions administered by U.S. agencies (Bureau of Industry and Security, Office of Foreign Assets Control)
Restrictions on movement between jurisdictions due to geopolitical events
Political instability, terrorism, and potential for hostilities in operating regions
